text: The surgeon performs a thymectomy using a right-sided approach with a three-port technique, including two 5mm ports and a 10mm port for the endoscope. The thymoma is clearly visible, and the surgeon uses a bipolar grasper and Maryland dissector to begin dissecting the thymus from the back of the sternum, maintaining a safe distance from the thymoma to ensure a good margin. The instruments are small and slim, suitable for precise dissection, and bipolar energy is preferred for its limited dissipation, especially near the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon begins dissection near the diaphragm, moving towards the pericardium while incorporating surrounding fat into the specimen. The right internal mammary artery is visible at the top of the screen, with dissection proceeding approximately one centimeter medial to it. The surgical setup includes a purple base unit with a bipolar instrument and a green base unit, while a cyan base unit is prepared but not utilized.

The surgical team uses five millimeter ports to facilitate instrument exchange, with the bedside assistant employing a harmonic device for vessel management. The surgeon dissects over the pericardium towards the left pleura, ensuring a large margin while avoiding the thymoma. The reamer is visible pulsating, and the dissection progresses under the sternum towards the left internal mammary artery. The right internal mammary vein is used as a landmark, visible near the Maryland dissector, as the pleura is carefully taken down.

The surgeon follows a path approximately half a centimeter medial to the phrenic nerve using bipolar energy, minimizing energy dissipation to protect the nerve. The superior vena cava is visible below the phrenic nerve, along with a lymph node that is included in the specimen. The surgeon peels the thymus from the pericardium, lifting forcefully with grasping forceps, and continues dissection along the phrenic nerve from below upwards. The clean pericardium is visible, free of fat.

The surgeon navigates the dissection into the neck area, following the innominate vein across. Visible landmarks include the white internal mammary vein and the superior vena cava (SVC). The surgeon identifies a potential vessel from the right internal mammary vein and addresses it using a harmonic device and a 5mm clip applicator, placing clips on either side of the vessel.

The surgeon uses the harmonic device to safely divide a small vein in the chest. The Maryland dissector is employed to carefully dissect the thymus from the innominate vein, revealing the vein just beyond the Maryland instrument. The thymic vein, which often drains into the innominate vein, is identified and prepared for clipping or division. The surgeon adheres to the principle of dissecting on named structures, avoiding fat to maintain orientation, while ensuring all soft tissue and fat are removed with the thymoma.

The surgeon uses the Versius robotic system to maneuver the camera and instruments, identifying potential vascular structures from the reamer vein or innominate vein to the thymus. The Maryland instrument is swapped out for a harmonic device to safely divide a vessel. The thymus's left and right superior horns are visible above the harmonic, and the surgeon pulls down on the thymus to extract it from the neck. The CO₂ insufflation creates space between the sternum and thymus, facilitating the division of the thymoma from the pericardium.

The surgeon tracks up into the right superior horn of the thymus, visualizing the innominate vein below the Maryland dissector. The thymic vein is identified emerging from the innominate vein, and the surgeon prepares to safely divide it by clipping the vein. The Maryland instrument is rotated and removed, and a harmonic device is introduced to divide the clipped thymic vein, demonstrating the ease of swapping instruments in the robotic system.

The surgeon uses Versys instruments to pull the right superior horn of the thymus briskly out of the neck, dividing the thyro-thymic ligament with a harmonic device. The left superior horn is then pulled out, staying medial to the pleura to protect the lemur artery and vein, while the assistant uses the harmonic for division.

The surgeon uses the Maryland instrument to retract the thymus into the right chest, dividing the left side of the thymus from the left pleura. The clean pericardium is visible below the Maryland, and small bits of tissue are separated, keeping the fat with the thymus. The left pleura is entered early in the procedure, and despite CO₂ insufflation, there is no issue with this approach. The left and right superior horns of the thymus are already removed from the neck, making the thymus more mobile.

The surgeon uses bipolar diathermy to dissect along the left pleura, joining the previous neck dissection. The fat beneath the left phrenic nerve is carefully pulled out, ensuring the nerve remains safe. The superior vena cava (SVC) is visible, with the innominate vein extending into the distance. The Maryland instrument is used to press the innominate vein while dissecting the left superior horn, dividing the thyrothymic ligament to complete the dissection.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the thymus, revealing the pericardium with the aorta beneath it, and the innominate and reamer veins devoid of fatty tissue. The superior vena cava (SVC) and phrenic nerve are clearly dissected. The thymoma, measuring seven centimeters, is placed into a retrieval bag through an enlarged right-sided port for removal.
